slides.title: Containerization with Docker
slides.content: Docker has fundamentally transformed modern software development by introducing a method to package applications along with their entire runtime dependencies, libraries, and configurations into a self-contained, immutable unit called a 'container.' Containers ensure consistent behavior across different environments, effectively eliminating the common 'it worked on my machine' friction by providing isolated, lightweight, and reproducible execution environments. Orchestrating these containers in complex, multi-component applications typically involves Docker Compose, a tool that simplifies defining and running multi-container Docker applications by utilizing a YAML-based configuration file to manage networking, volumes, environment variables, and inter-service dependencies. This approach enables developers to simulate complex production environmentslocally, accelerates onboarding, streamlines testing, and facilitates seamless scalability in production environments through orchestration platforms. By abstracting the complexities of infrastructure from the application logic, containerization enables teams to deploy services more frequently, with higher confidence, directly supporting modern DevOps practices, microservices architectures, and continuous integration/continuous deployment pipelines with unparalleled efficiency and consistency, regardless of the underlying cloud provider or configuration.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f0fdf4'/><rect x='80' y='60' width='240' height='80' fill='#22c55e' fill-opacity='0.2' rx='10'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#166534'>Immutable Containerized Units</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Explain how Docker containers eliminate environment-related bugs during deployment.
How is networking between containers managed within a Docker Compose configuration?
Describe the role of volume mapping in maintaining persistent data for ephemeral containers.

slides.title: Automated Deployment with Coolify
slides.content: Coolify provides a self-hosted, open-source alternative to proprietary CI/CD and PaaS platforms—like Heroku or Vercel—allowing developers to host, deploy, and manage their own production infrastructure with minimal management overhead. Coolify excels in orchestrating complex deployments by automatically configuring build processes, managing webhooks for seamless CI/CD integration, and handling necessary infrastructure for multi-language, containerized applications. When paired with CI/CD tools, Coolify enables automated rebuilding and redeployment of services upon each git commit or branch merge, reducing manual intervention in the delivery pipeline. By leveraging these platforms, engineering teams can focus almost exclusively on shipping new functionality rather than managing the complexities of provisioning, patching, or scaling remote servers. Coolify’s intuitive dashboard simplifies environment management, monitoring, and scaling of containerized microservices, offering a powerful, scalable approach to building production-ready deployment pipelines while maintaining full control over the infrastructure, security, and cost, which is essential for both startups and large-scale enterprises transitioning to modern, cloud-native DevOps practices.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fffbeb'/><path d='M100 50 L300 50 L200 150 Z' fill='#fbbf24'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#92400e'>Automated Deployment Orchestration</text></svg>
studentPrompts: How can webhooks be used to trigger automated deployment workflows upon new code commits?
Compare the functionality of Coolify as a PaaS to proprietary solutions like Vercel or Heroku.
What are the benefits of self-hosting your own CI/CD orchestration infrastructure?
